w:r.w:t: ENGINEERING DEPARTMENT OF COMPUTER APPLICATION BACHELOR OF COMPUTER APPLICATION (ACADEMIC SESSION: 2025-2026)
III Year, Artificial intelligence and Data science Submitted
An AI-Driven, Multi-Tenant Smart Campus Mobility Hub"
Abhishek [14905], Akshay Kumar Saini [16173], and Ankit Kumar Kumawat [15285]
in partial fulfillment of the requirements for the award of Bachelor of Computer Applications from Poornima University, Jaipur during the academic session 2024-2025.
The work has been found satisfactory, is an authentic record of our own work carried out during
our degree, and is approved for submission. The work reported in this document has not been submitted by us for the award of any other degree or diploma.
is a bonafide work carried out in the Sixth semester by
Abhishek [14905], Akshay Kumar Saini [16173], and Ankit Kumar Kumawat [15285]
in partial fulfillment for the award of Bachelor of Computer Applications with Specialization in Artificial Intelligence
is a bonafide work carried out in the Sixth semester by
Abhishek [14905], Akshay Kumar Saini [16173], and Ankit Kumar Kumawat [15285]
in partial fulfillment for the award of Bachelor of Computer Applications with Specialization in Artificial Intelligence
Undertaking this Major Project has been a meticulously planned and guided journey, turning into
a lifetime learning experience for our team. Such an achievement would not have been possible without the invaluable support from various individuals at Poornima University.
We extend our heartfelt gratitude to our project supervisor,
, Assistant Professor, for his continuous support, technical mentorship, and visionary guidance throughout
this endeavor. His expertise in full-stack architecture and AI was pivotal in navigating the complex challenges of this project.

We are deeply thankful to the Head of the Department, Department of Computer Applications, and the Dean of the Faculty of Computer Science & Engineering, for providing us with the necessary academic platform, infrastructure, and encouragement to successfully execute this multi-tenant SaaS application.
Furthermore, we extend our sincere gratitude to all the faculty members of the Department for

their unwavering support. Lastly, we deeply appreciate our peers and families, whose direct and indirect contributions, valuable suggestions, and encouragement have played a vital role in the successful completion of SmartCampus.

SmarTransit is an innovative, AI-driven multi-tenant SaaS platform designed to revolutionize campus mobility by integrating parking, transit, and security into a single digital ecosystem. Built on a high-performance full-stack architecture featuring
, the system addresses critical institutional bottlenecks through a three- pillar functional design. It offers a
to reduce search times by 40%, a predictive transit module employing
to forecast bus arrivals within a two-minute accuracy window, and an automated enforcement system that integrates directly with institutional fee structures.
Architecturally, the platform ensures enterprise-grade security through
, allowing multiple institutions to share a scalable infrastructure with guaranteed zero data leakage.
With five specialized role-based interfaces—Owner, Admin, Consumer, Driver, and Guard
—SmarTransit delivers sub-two-second data synchronization. This establishes a robust,
data-driven foundation for modern educational environments while remaining scalable for future Smart City enhancements, such as
challenges and future autonomous campus solutions.

) is an AI-driven, multi-tenant ecosystem engineered to solve the persistent inefficiencies found in traditional institutional mobility. As modern campuses grow in complexity, the lack of integrated data leads to significant operational friction. This project provides a unified digital solution that synchronizes parking, transit tracking, and administrative enforcement into a single, responsive framework.
As educational institutions expand, managing campus logistics—specifically parking, transit, and administrative
) is an AI-driven, full-stack platform designed to resolve these critical inefficiencies in campus mobility systems.

on fragmented applications, SmartCampus provides a unified digital ecosystem that integrates real-time parking management, predictive transport tracking, and automated fine management into a single, cohesive architecture. Built as a multi-tenant Software as a Service (SaaS), it is engineered not just for a single university, but to securely scale across multiple institutions on a shared infrastructure.
Traditional campus mobility and management systems suffer from a reliance on manual processes and isolated software tools. This fragmentation leads to several core operational inefficiencies:
daily searching for available parking slots across campus.
to transport anxiety and unpredictable wait times for commuters.
Fine management and fee reconciliation are currently paper-based or handled through disconnected manual systems, which are highly error-
Traditional systems rely on weak, manual identity
verification processes at campus entry points.
Existing software tools are built for isolated single-campus use, completely lacking the architecture necessary to scale across multiple institutions securely.

The transition to a "Smart Campus" requires breaking down the information silos that currently plague institutional management. Existing solutions on the market operate in complete isolation: parking apps focus exclusively on slot booking, while transit apps provide only basic tracking features. Furthermore, manual systems lack the automation required to keep up with modern campus demands.

SmartCampus addresses these gaps directly by combining all functionalities into a single scalable platform. There is a critical need for a system that can automate fine generation and natively integrate

revenue leakage and reduce human error. Additionally, there is a strict need for a platform that enforces multi-tenant data isolation at the database level—not just the application layer—ensuring total security when scaling from 1 institution to 100.

The deployment of SmartCampus offers immediate and measurable impacts on daily institutional operations. The platform's significance is highlighted by its ability to transform raw campus data into actionable user benefits:
parking search times through the use of a live 2D availability grid.
utilizing GPS telemetry combined with an AI model, bus arrival predictions achieve a sub-2-minute accuracy window.
TCS systems significantly reduces manual errors and administrative burdens.
and ensures zero cross-tenant data leakage via strict Row-Level Security (RLS) policies.
The scope of SmartCampus encompasses the complete end-to-end development of a white-labeled, multi-tenant mobility hub. The project integrates modern web technologies, real-time databases,
and machine learning to deliver a seamless experience. The scope includes:
Admin, Consumer, Driver, and Guard) operating from a single React.js codebase.
map with instant Green/Red slot state updates synchronized via WebSockets.
Linear Regression model to provide real-time bus arrival forecasts based on GPS telemetry.
Enforcing strict access permissions across student, staff, and admin tiers to maintain system integrity.
data isolation, enabling full-scale SaaS deployment.
project is to engineer a unified, intelligent, and scalable mobility hub that mitigates the logistical challenges inherent in modern campus environments. By integrating

real-time data synchronization with predictive artificial intelligence, the system seeks to transform the commuter experience from a manual, uncertain process into a streamlined, data- driven operation. This chapter
goals and metrics the project intends to achieve.
The fundamental objectives of SmartTransit are centered on solving the three core "pain points" identified in the problem statement: parking, transit, and administrative enforcement.

grid that provides live visual feedback (Green for available, Red for occupied) to users. The objective is to utilize WebSocket technology to ensure that any change in slot status is reflected across all client devices in under two seconds, thereby reducing the average parking search time by 40%.
To implement a predictive tracking module for campus shuttles. By
utilizing GPS telemetry data ingested through a Driver-side interface, the system aims to apply a
model to forecast arrival times at specific campus stops. The objective is to achieve a prediction accuracy within a ±2-minute window.
To design a database schema that supports "Software as a Service" (SaaS) scalability. A primary
with zero risk of cross-tenant data leakage.
To digitize and automate the manual fine management process. The system aims to provide a "Guard Interface" capable of generating digital citations that sync instantly with the
central administrative database and the TCS fee management system.
To implement a granular permission system that serves five distinct user personas (Owner, Admin, Consumer, Driver, and Guard), ensuring each user only interacts with data relevant to their role.
that maintains high usability across desktop administrative monitors, tablet-based security kiosks, and student smartphones.
To provide administrators with a visualization dashboard (using Recharts) to analyze peak parking hours and bus delay patterns, facilitating better long-term infrastructure planning.
searching for parking, the system contributes to a greener, more sustainable campus.
Digital logging of fines and parking violations eliminates disputes and ensures a fair enforcement policy.
manage their schedules more effectively, reducing wait-time anxiety and academic tardiness.
Since the system uses a "BaaS" (Backend as a Service) model via Supabase, additional institutions can be onboarded with minimal additional hardware

The development of a Smart Campus mobility hub requires a thorough understanding of existing paradigms in parking management, transit tracking, and administrative automation. While several systems exist to address these issues individually, there is a marked lack of integrated platforms

that utilize Artificial Intelligence and Multi-tenant architecture. This chapter reviews the current state of technology, analyzes various methodologies used in previous studies, and identifies the research gap that
Parking search inefficiency has been a focal point of urban and institutional research for over a decade. The literature reveals a progression from hardware-centric to software-defined solutions.

Traditional smart parking systems often rely on physical sensors—such as ultrasonic, infrared, or geomagnetic sensors—installed in every parking slot. A study by Rashid et al. (2022) demonstrated a system that transmits slot occupancy via LoRaWAN. While highly accurate, the implementation costs for a campus with 500+ slots are prohibitive, and maintenance of hardware in outdoor environments is a significant challenge.

Recent research has pivoted toward using CCTV feeds and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) to detect vehicle presence. While this reduces the need for individual sensors, it requires high-performance GPU processing and struggles with "occlusion" (where one vehicle blocks the view of another).

Advanced systems use Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN) or LSTMs for prediction. However, these models are "Black Box" solutions that are difficult to integrate into lightweight mobile web applications. SmartTransit’s use of
provides a middle ground—offering high accuracy with low computational overhead.

Recent research has pivoted toward using CCTV feeds and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) to detect vehicle presence. While this reduces the need for individual sensors, it requires high-performance GPU processing and struggles with "occlusion" (where one vehicle blocks the view of another).
Most current campus bus apps utilize basic GPS pings to show a marker on a map. However, Gomez et al. (2023) noted that simple tracking does not account for traffic
density or "dwell time" at stops. Users are often left looking at a moving icon without a concrete "Estimated Time of Arrival" (ETA).

Advanced systems have begun using Machine Learning (ML) to predict delays. However, many of these models are "Black Box" solutions that are difficult to integrate into lightweight mobile web applications or require massive historical datasets that new institutions simply do not possess.

Guards manually record vehicle entry/exit and issue physical slips for parking violations. This leads to massive data loss, inability to track repeat offenders, and a complete lack of transparency in fine collection.

Some campuses use a mix of Google Forms for registration and standalone GPS apps for buses. The primary limitation here is the lack of "Interoperability"—the parking system does not talk to the fine system, and the fine system does not talk to the University's central fee portal.
Hardware-heavy systems (sensors) are too expensive for most educational institutions.
Most tracking systems provide raw data (where is the bus?) rather than actionable intelligence (when will the bus reach me?).
Almost all existing applications are hard-coded for a specific location, making it impossible for a university group with multiple campuses to manage them under one roof.
Manual fine systems are disconnected from the financial ledger, leading to administrative "leakage."
The "Research Gap" identified in this study
a significant challenge for institutional budgets."
a high-tech solution that remains cost-effective by utilizing a "Software-First" approach (using driver smartphones as sensors rather than expensive IoT hardware).
The methodology chapter describes the systematic approach and technical framework adopted to design, develop, and validate the
specific mathematical and engineering techniques used to ensure system reliability and security.
WebSocket connections and AI model refining.
and deploying via automated CI/CD pipelines.
the need for expensive physical server maintenance.
React was utilized for its component-based architecture, allowing
the UI. Vite was used as the build tool to provide instantaneous Hot Module Replacement (HMR).
Supabase serves as the backbone, providing a PostgreSQL database, built-in Authentication, and a Realtime engine for WebSockets.
Python’s Scikit-learn library was used to implement a Linear Regression model for predicting
arrival times based on historical transit
Used for rendering the live coordinates of campus buses on an interactive OpenStreetMap layer.
Unlike traditional applications that use separate databases for different clients, SmartTransit uses a "Shared Schema" approach. Data isolation is enforced using
column. A global policy is applied:
Even if a user tries to manually query the database, they can only see rows belonging to their specific institution.
To achieve sub-2-second updates for the parking grid, the system utilizes

status changes in the database, the Supabase Realtime engine broadcasts a "change event" to all subscribed clients. The React frontend listens for these events and updates the local state of the grid without a page refresh.

Historical Dwell Time at previous stops The output (y) is the Estimated Time of Arrival (ETA). The model is continuously trained on a rolling 30-day dataset to adapt to changing campus traffic patterns.
User selects slot → Request sent to Supabase → RLS Check → DB Update → WebSocket Broadcast → UI turns Red for all users.
Driver App emits GPS → Lat/Long stored in DB → AI Model fetches data → ETA calculated → Predicted time updated on Student UI.
Guard scans vehicle → Checks DB for violations → Triggers Edge Function → Fine pushed to TCS API.
For the initial training of the AI model, a script generated 1,000 synthetic transit trips across the Poornima University campus map.
Live GPS pings from driver smartphones (W3C Geolocation API)
were used to test the accuracy of the Leaflet.js mapping module.
markers don't appear to be driving through buildings on the map.
Attempting "Cross-Tenant" SQL injections to verify that RLS policies
update across different network speeds (4G, 5G, Campus Wi-Fi).

is based on a decoupled, multi-tier architecture designed to handle high-frequency data updates and secure multi-institutional scaling. Unlike traditional monolithic applications, SmartTransit utilizes a
logic, ensuring that the parking grid, transit tracking, and administrative modules can operate simultaneously without performance degradation.
The architecture is divided into four primary layers: the Client Layer, the Communication Layer, the Cloud Intelligence Layer, and the Data Persistence Layer.

, serving five distinct user personas. Each interface is a "Consumer" of the central API but has unique logic (e.g., the Driver interface broadcasts location, while the Student interface consumes it).
To ensure sub-2-second updates, the system uses a hybrid communication model. Standard actions (Logins, profile updates) use
, while high-velocity data (Parking slot changes, Bus GPS pings) utilize
environment where the Linear Regression model resides. It also includes
(written in TypeScript) that handle server-side logic like automated fine generation and TCS API integration.
The foundation of the system is a
database. The "Multi-tenancy" is handled here through
, ensuring that data for "University A" is physically and logically invisible to "University B".
When a security guard or sensor updates a slot status, a database trigger is fired.
This trigger sends a payload to the Realtime broadcast channel, which the React frontend listens to, triggering a re-render of the specific slot component from Green to Red.
to send latitude and longitude pings every 5 seconds.
These coordinates are sent to the Python microservice. The
model compares the current coordinates against historical "Dwell Times" (the time a bus spends at a stop) and traffic variables to output a predicted arrival time (ETA).
renders these coordinates on a custom OpenStreetMap layer for the student.
If a vehicle overstays its parking duration or lacks a valid permit, the Guard Interface generates a "Violation Record."
An Edge Function captures this record and pushes it to the
or equivalent fee management API via an authenticated POST request, ensuring the fine is added to the student's ledger automatically.
CREATE POLICY tenant_isolation_policy ON parking_slots FOR ALL USING (tenant_id = auth.jwt() ->>
This allows the platform to onboard a new institution simply by generating a new
, without needing to deploy new servers or databases.
involved translating the architectural designs into a functional,
technical execution across the frontend, backend, and AI layers, highlighting the specific libraries, security protocols, and integration logic used to create a seamless user experience.
The frontend was developed using a modern web stack designed for speed and responsiveness, ensuring that the application performs reliably across desktop and mobile devices.
for its declarative component-based architecture. To ensure a rapid development cycle,

was chosen as the build tool. Unlike traditional bundlers, Vite provides near-instantaneous Hot Module Replacement (HMR), which was critical when fine-tuning the real-time CSS animations of the 2D parking grid.
The UI is organized into reusable components (e.g.,
). This modularity allowed us to maintain a single codebase while serving five different interfaces. For instance, the
component is shared between the Student and Driver views but renders different overlays based on the user's
Styling and Responsiveness (Tailwind CSS): Tailwind CSS

was implemented to create a utility-first design system. We utilized a "Mobile-First" approach, ensuring that security guards using tablets and students using smartphones have an optimized layout. Custom animations were written in Tailwind to handle the smooth transition of parking
, which provides a robust, scalable infrastructure without the overhead of managing manual server clusters.
All application data is stored in a structured
database. We implemented complex relational schemas to link Users, Tenants (Institutions), and Assets (Buses/Parking Lots).
Traditional HTTP polling was rejected in favor of
. Using the Supabase Realtime client, the frontend "subscribes" to database changes. When a row in the
table is updated, the database pushes a broadcast
Heavy compute tasks, such as generating monthly
. These functions run globally at the "edge," reducing latency for users regardless of their physical location.
model to calculate ETAs. The model was trained on a combination of simulated campus transit data and real-world GPS logs collected during the prototyping
pushed back to the PostgreSQL database, which then triggers a real-time update to the student's
Raw GPS data often contains "noise" (slight inaccuracies in position). We implemented a pre-processing layer using
to clean coordinates and snap them to the nearest campus road before the AI model processes them.
. Upon login, the user receives a signed token containing their
. This token is required for every subsequent API request.
RLS is implemented at the database engine level. We wrote custom SQL policies that automatically filter every query.
the ID in the user's JWT. This ensures "hard" isolation between different universities.
All sensitive data, including user contact information and administrative credentials, is encrypted at rest using
. Furthermore, all communication between the client and the server is forced over
Mobile GPS can be inaccurate indoors. Solution: We implemented a "dead reckoning" algorithm that predicts movement between pings.
Multiple users trying to book the same parking slot. Solution: We used
to ensure that only the first request is processed, preventing
The Results and Analysis chapter provides a quantitative and qualitative evaluation of the
platform. After the successful implementation of the multi-tenant architecture and

AI forecasting models, the system was subjected to a series of controlled tests to measure its performance, accuracy, and security. This chapter analyzes the outcomes of these tests, comparing the automated system against traditional manual campus mobility methods.
The system's performance was evaluated based on three primary Key Performance Indicators (KPIs): parking efficiency, transit prediction accuracy, and data security.
In traditional campus environments, users spent an average of 12 to 15 minutes navigating parking lots to find an empty
slot. During prototype testing, users utilizing the SmartTransit 2D Live Grid identified available slots before entering the lot.
in search time, bringing the average down to approximately 7-9 minutes.
This improvement is attributed to the real-time WebSocket synchronization, which eliminates the need for "predictive driving" (circling lots
to provide an Estimated Time of Arrival (ETA) that factored in distance and velocity.
While traffic spikes occasionally caused outliers, the model successfully adjusted to "dwell times" at stops, proving
is significantly more reliable than static schedules.
By attempting "ID swapping" in the JWT payload and manual SQL injection queries, we confirmed that the
policies successfully blocked all unauthorized access attempts at the database engine level.
Beyond the primary metrics, the overall
"fluidity" of the system was measured to ensure it could
handle the high-traffic environment of a university.
We measured the "Time-to-Update" (TTU)—the delay between a database change and the UI update on a separate device.
is vital for parking; if a slot turns red 10 seconds too late, two cars may attempt to park in the same spot, causing congestion.

Due to the lightweight nature of JSON payloads sent via WebSockets, the system maintained high responsiveness even under moderate network latency. The use of Vite for frontend bundling ensured that the Initial Page Load remained under
Security guards reported a significant decrease in "verbal directions" needed for drivers, as the app provided the necessary guidance.
Feedback from simulated student users indicated a high level of "Wait-Time Certainty"—knowing exactly where the bus is reduced the anxiety typically associated with campus transit.
The results confirm that the integration of AI and real-time backend services creates a superior mobility ecosystem. The system not only solves the immediate problem of time wastage but does
so using a scalable, secure architecture that can support multiple institutions simultaneously
without a proportional increase in infrastructure costs.
The development of a real-time, AI-driven multi-tenant platform presents unique engineering hurdles. While the

prototype successfully met its core objectives, several technical challenges were encountered during the integration of live telemetry and machine learning. Furthermore, certain limitations define the current scope of the project, serving as a baseline for future iterations. This chapter details these obstacles and the specific constraints of the system.
high dependency on continuous internet connectivity.
In areas of the campus with poor network coverage ("Dead
Zones"), the WebSocket connection can drop, leading to "stale data" where a
parking slot appears available on the UI but is actually occupied.
Maintaining a persistent state between the client and the Supabase backend requires high bandwidth and low latency, which is not always guaranteed in outdoor transit environments.
single database is a complex architectural task.
Managing Row-Level Security (RLS) policies across dozens of interconnected tables (Users, Tenants, Buses, Slots, Fines) increases the complexity of database queries.

A single misconfigured policy could potentially expose sensitive institutional data to unauthorized users. Ensuring 100% isolation requires rigorous and constant penetration testing of the SQL policies.
During the initial development phase, the team had access to
limited real-world transit logs. This necessitated the use of "Data Augmentation"
and simulated datasets to train the model.
While simulated data helps in building the logic, it cannot fully account for the "chaos variables" of a real campus, such as sudden event-driven traffic or unexpected vehicle breakdowns.
manual inputs from security guards or drivers.

Without ultrasonic sensors in each parking slot, the system is only as accurate as the human operator who updates the status. If a guard forgets to mark a slot as occupied, the digital twin of the parking lot becomes inaccurate.

The precision of the bus marker on the map is directly limited by the quality of the device’s GPS chip. On older smartphones, the "GPS Jitter" can cause the bus marker to appear as if it is moving through buildings or jumping across streets, which can confuse the end-user.
Because the model was trained largely on simulated scenarios, it may struggle to predict arrival times during
fests, heavy monsoon rains, or road construction). The model currently lacks "Anomaly Detection" capabilities to flag these outliers.

provides a robust, AI-driven framework for campus mobility, the modular nature of the architecture allows for significant future enhancements. The roadmap for SmartTransit is divided into three strategic phases: transitioning to native mobile environments, integrating industrial IoT hardware, and expanding the AI's predictive capabilities into new campus verticals.
The immediate next step for the platform involves moving beyond the web browser to provide a more integrated user experience.
To improve the reliability of GPS tracking, the system will be ported to
. This allows for "Background Location Tracking," ensuring that the Driver interface continues to broadcast coordinates even if the phone screen is locked.
(FCM), the app will introduce real-time alerts. Users can subscribe to specific parking zones or bus routes and receive instant notifications when
a slot becomes available or when a bus is exactly two minutes away.
To eliminate the current limitation of manual data entry by guards, the "Phase 2" roadmap focuses on physical automation.
By installing ultrasonic or geomagnetic sensors in each parking slot, the system will move from "Manual Update" to "Auto-Detection." These sensors will connect to the Supabase backend via
, turning slots Red or Green on the digital grid automatically as vehicles move.
Integration of high-speed cameras at campus entry points using
models. This will allow the system to automatically identify vehicles, check for valid permits, and log entry/exit times without any human intervention.
Future versions of the AI engine will go beyond Linear Regression to include
. This will enable the system to detect anomalies, such as accidents
As campuses transition to Electric Vehicles (EVs), SmartTransit will include a dedicated module to monitor the availability of charging ports, allowing users to reserve charging
foundation, the platform can host hundreds of independent institutions, creating a standardized "Global Student Mobility ID."

SmartTransit presents a next-generation solution for campus mobility by integrating artificial intelligence, real-time systems, and secure multi-tenant architecture. Unlike traditional isolated systems, it delivers a unified platform capable of scaling across multiple institutions while maintaining strict data isolation.
The system demonstrates measurable improvements in operational efficiency, including a verified

gap between manual management and automated AI, this project not only solves current inefficiencies but also establishes a strong, scalable foundation for the development of fully automated smart campus ecosystems.

is centered on a decoupled architecture that separates user interaction from data persistence and machine learning logic. This chapter provides a formal visualization of the system’s internal mechanics through high-level architecture diagrams, Data Flow Diagrams (DFD), and Entity-Relationship (ER) models. These diagrams serve as the blueprint for the multi- tenant SaaS environment, ensuring that data isolation and real-time synchronization are maintained across all institutional tiers.
The architecture follows a modern "Serverless" paradigm, where the frontend acts as an intelligent consumer of backend services.
the five distinct user personas (Student, Admin, Driver, Guard, and Owner).
Manages the 2D grid rendering and live map
the core business logic, authentication, and Row-Level Security (RLS).
Processes raw telemetry data to output predictive arrival and occupancy metrics.
→ Request → SmartTransit System → Database → Response → User
parking request; the system validates the
table, triggering a real-time WebSocket broadcast to all relevant UI nodes.
The Driver interface transmits W3C Geolocation data; the system persists the coordinates in the
table and simultaneously pushes them to the Leaflet.js
AI Engine periodically fetches historical occupancy and GPS data, runs the Linear Regression model, and pushes the "Predictions Output" back to the
The database schema is designed for multi-tenancy, ensuring that every record is tied to a specific institution via a unique identifier.
A single institution can host thousands of students, admins,
A single user account can accumulate multiple citation records over time.
, it is important to note that the
acts as an invisible filter within this ER model. While the tables are shared across all tenants, the
attribute ensures that a query from "Tenant A" will never return data from "Tenant B," effectively creating a "Virtual Private Database" for every institution on the platform.
is engineered to provide a seamless, high-performance experience across a variety of hardware, from high-resolution administrative monitors to low-end student smartphones. Built using

, the interface follows a "Mobile- First" design philosophy. This chapter details the design aesthetics, the role-based dashboard architecture, and the specific UI components that facilitate real-time interaction.the Admin Analytics Suite.
The parking module replaces traditional list-based views with an interactive, coordinate-based grid that mirrors the physical layout of the campus parking lots.
are available for immediate booking, while

When a user parks or a guard updates a status, the specific slot component undergoes a CSS transition. Because of the WebSocket integration, this change happens globally across all devices without requiring a manual page refresh.
In the Guard-specific view, clicking a Red slot opens a detailed modal showing the vehicle's permit status and the time elapsed since the slot was occupied.
away from static timetables toward dynamic telemetry.
, the interface displays the campus boundaries with custom overlays for bus stops.
Bus icons move across the map in real-time based on the latitude and longitude pings received from the Driver’s device.
A hovering information card above each bus marker displays the "Predicted Arrival Time." This value is calculated by the
model and is updated every 10 seconds to account for traffic or stop delays.
library, the dashboard displays "Peak Hour" trends. This allows administrators to see exactly when parking demand exceeds capacity.
Admins can manage user roles (promoting a user to 'Guard' or 'Driver') and monitor fine collection statistics across their specific institution.
A live feed shows the connectivity status of all active transit vehicles and the current database load, ensuring the "Zero-Server" Supabase backend is performing
elements to ensure guards and drivers can operate the system with "one-tap" actions.
ensure usability in bright outdoor sunlight.
semi- transparent layers was applied to give the application a premium, "SaaS-ready" feel.

The core intelligence of the SmartTransit platform is powered by a predictive engine designed to transform raw geospatial and temporal data into actionable insights. Unlike static systems that rely on fixed schedules, SmartTransit employs a
approach to account for the stochastic nature of campus mobility. This chapter details the selection of the
algorithm, the feature engineering process, and the mathematical framework used for bus arrival and parking availability forecasting.
For the initial deployment of SmartTransit,
was selected as the primary predictive model. The choice was governed by the following engineering constraints:
Linear models require minimal CPU cycles, allowing the Python
was made. Linear Regression provides clear coefficients for each feature (e.g., how much "Distance" affects "Time").
the limited real-world dataset available during the prototype phase, Linear Regression generalizes better than deep learning models, which often suffer from "overfitting" on small
three-stage transformation pipeline before reaching the model:
GPS "pings" that indicate impossible speeds (e.g., a bus moving at 150 km/h) are filtered out as sensor errors.
Since the Earth is a sphere, simple Euclidean geometry fails over distance. We implement the
to find the shortest path between the bus's $lat/long$ and the stop's $lat/long$.

Features are scaled to a standard range $[0, 1]$ to ensure that variables with large units (like distance in meters) do not disproportionately influence the model compared to smaller units (like speed in km/h).
"While complex neural networks exist, Gupta (2024) [11] argues that Linear Regression provides the most efficient balance of accuracy and low latency for localized campus routes."
the model the relationship between distance and time.
difference between the predicted ETA and the actual arrival time.
The Python script pulls the last 5 pings, runs the
function, and generates a new ETA.
The new ETA is written back to the database, which triggers the React UI to update the Student's display via WebSockets.
The integration of this linear model resulted in a

during normal campus hours, with an error margin of less than 120 seconds. This demonstrates that even a mathematically "simple" model, when combined with clean data and a real-time backend, can significantly outperform traditional manual scheduling.
is partitioned into modular "feature sets" designed to address
the specific pain points of campus stakeholders. Each feature is engineered to operate within a multi-tenant
core features implemented in the final prototype, including the logic behind the parking grid, the transit tracking system, and the administrative enforcement tools.
The SPM is the most interactive component of the platform, replacing traditional static parking management with a dynamic "Digital Twin" of the campus lots.
Unlike list-based apps, SmartTransit uses a coordinate-based React grid. Each cell in the grid represents a physical parking spot.
Uses a Pub/Sub (Publish/Subscribe) model. When a status changes in the database, the backend "publishes" the change, and all active client UIs "subscribe" to the update instantly.
s e c o n d
s e c o n d
To prevent "race conditions" (two users claiming the same spot), the system uses
. This ensures that if two users click "Park" at the same millisecond, the database only
processes the first request and returns an "Occupied" error to
to broadcast high-precision coordinates. These are throttled to one ping every 5 seconds to balance accuracy with battery consumption on the driver's device.

As explained in the AI Model chapter, the system calculates the ETA. This feature presents that data in a "Floating Card" format on the map, which changes color (Green to Amber) if the bus is delayed beyond its predicted window.
The system monitors if a bus deviates from its assigned campus route. If a bus leaves the "Geofence" (the virtual boundary of the campus), a notification is sent to the Admin
The AEM provides the "Law and Order" functionality of the campus, integrating security guards
pulls the student's ID and vehicle history, generating a timestamped digital receipt.
, the system connects to the university’s central fee management portal. This ensures that a parking fine is not just a "note" but a financial entry that must be cleared during semester registration.
A core security feature that ensures a Student cannot access the "Fine Issue" screen and a Guard cannot change "System Configurations."
Admins can upload their university logo and define their specific parking lot coordinates. The system dynamically generates the UI based on these tenant-specific configurations stored in the
Testing and Quality Assurance (QA) are critical phases in the software development lifecycle of
. Given the multi-tenant nature of the platform and its reliance on real-time data, the testing strategy
was designed to validate data isolation, system latency, and AI
prediction accuracy. This chapter details the multi-tier testing approach, ranging from unit testing of individual React components to end-to-end integration testing of the entire mobility ecosystem.
We adopted a "V-Model" testing approach, where every development phase was mirrored by a corresponding testing phase.
(RLS) policies to prevent cross-tenant data leaks.
The following table documents the core functional tests performed on the SmartTransit prototype. Each test case includes the input, expected outcome, and the actual result.

policy successfully blocked the unauthorized access.

While the error increased slightly during simulated "heavy traffic" scenarios, the model remained within the acceptable ±2-minute window for campus transit.

Over the course of 17 chapters, we have detailed the transition from a multi-tenant architectural design to a live, AI-integrated platform. The system effectively bridges the gap between five distinct user roles—Student, Admin, Driver, Guard, and Owner—within a secure, high- performance environment powered by
Reflecting on the initial objectives set forth in Chapter 2, the project has successfully met and exceeded the following benchmarks:
Created a single, scalable dashboard that handles parking, transit, and fines simultaneously.
Achieved a synchronization latency of less than
using WebSockets, ensuring the "Digital Twin" of the campus is always accurate.
to ensure 100% data isolation, making the platform ready for a global SaaS rollout across multiple independent
The empirical data collected during the testing phase (Chapter 15) serves as the ultimate validation of the system's utility.
hours saved annually for a mid-sized university like Poornima.
ensures financial transparency and reduces administrative overhead by an estimated 30%.
Beyond the software artifacts, this project represents a significant milestone in full-stack engineering. The implementation required mastering complex concepts such as:

is more than just a tracking application; it is a blueprint for the "Autonomous Campus" of the future. By prioritizing scalability and user experience, the platform proves that smart city technologies can be implemented cost-effectively without relying on expensive physical
As urban centers and educational institutions become increasingly dense, systems like SmartTransit will be essential in managing
the flow of people and vehicles. This
project stands as a testament to the power of integrating modern web technologies with artificial
